sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Eventlog Clear or Configuration Using Wevtutil
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects clearing or configuration of eventlogs using wevtutil, powershell and wmic. Might be used by ransomwares during the attack (seen by NotPetya and others).

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: COM Hijacking For Persistence With Suspicious Locations
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects potential COM object hijacking where the "Server" (In/Out) is pointing to a supsicious or unsuale location
Adversaries may abuse Visual Basic (VB) for execution
Detects process creation events that use the Sysnative folder (common for CobaltStrike spawns)
Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Disable Microsoft Defender Firewall via Registry
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Adversaries may disable or modify system firewalls in order to bypass controls limiting network usage
Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code

Adversaries may employ various system checks to detect and avoid virtualization and analysis environments. This may include changing behaviors based on the results of checks for the presence of artifacts indicative of a virtual machine environment (VME) or sandbox

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Windows Registry Persistence COM Search Order Hijacking
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects potential COM object hijacking leveraging the COM Search Order
Adversaries may install a root certificate on a compromised system to avoid warnings when connecting to adversary controlled web servers.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an Adobe Type 1 Font. The Type 1 Font Format is a standardized font format for digital imaging applications.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious New Instance Of An Office COM Object
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an svchost process spawning an instance of an office application. This happens when the initial word application creates an instance of one of the Office COM objects such as 'Word.Application', 'Excel.Application', etc.
This can be used by malicious actors to create malicious Office documents with macros on the fly. (See vba2clr project in the references)

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects a PDF file that contains JavaScript. JavaScript can be used to customize PDFs by implementing objects, methods, and properties. While not inherently malicious, embedding JavaScript inside of a PDF is often used for malicious purposes such as malware delivery or exploitation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS.full_description: Allows the app to show notifications

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the app to control the vibrator.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description:
Allows the app to
        have itself started as soon as the system has finished booting.
        This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the
        app to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view information
      about Wi-Fi networking, such as whether Wi-Fi is enabled and name of
      connected Wi-Fi devices.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows the app to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE.full_description: Allows the app to make use of foreground services.

android.permission.FLASHLIGHT.full_description: Allows the app to control the flashlight.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.short_description: access approximate location only in the foreground
android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.full_description: This app can get your approximate location from location services while the app is in use. Location services for your device must be turned on for the app to get location.

android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view information
      about Wi-Fi networking, such as whether Wi-Fi is enabled and name of
      connected Wi-Fi devices.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.EXPAND_STATUS_BAR.full_description: Allows the app to expand or collapse the status bar.

android.permission.USE_BIOMETRIC.full_description: Allows the app to use biometric hardware for authentication

android.permission.USE_FINGERPRINT.full_description: Allows the app to use fingerprint hardware for authentication

android.permission.REORDER_TASKS.full_description:
Allows the app to move tasks to the
      foreground and background.  The app may do this without your input.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.short_description: modify or delete the contents of your shared storage
android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.full_description: Allows the app to write the contents of your shared storage.

android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS.full_description: Allows the app to show notifications

android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.short_description: access precise location only in the foreground
android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.full_description: This app can get your precise location from location services while the app is in use. Location services for your device must be turned on for the app to get location. This may increase battery usage.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description:
Allows the app to
        have itself started as soon as the system has finished booting.
        This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the
        app to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.READ_PHONE_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to access the phone
      features of the device.  This permission allows the app to determine the
      phone number and device IDs, whether a call is active, and the remote number
      connected by a call.

android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE.full_description: Allows the app to make use of foreground services.

android.permission.SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM.full_description: This app can schedule work to happen at a desired time in the future. This also means that the app can run when youu2019re not actively using the device.

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the app to control the vibrator.

android.permission.CAMERA.full_description: This app can take pictures and record videos using the camera while the app is in use.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows the app to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

names: Cybercrime and Espionage_ An Analysis of Subversive Multi-Vector Threats.pdf
attributes.meaningful_name: Cybercrime and Espionage_ An Analysis of Subversive Multi-Vector Threats.pdf

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects a PDF file that contains JavaScript. JavaScript can be used to customize PDFs by implementing objects, methods, and properties. While not inherently malicious, embedding JavaScript inside of a PDF is often used for malicious purposes such as malware delivery or exploitation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Synthesis of the two reports indicates the PDF is benign. The internal technical analysis identified a large, structurally complex file with numerous decoding errors and a high count of URI objects. However, it critically found no evidence of a malicious execution chain, such as automatic action triggers or embedded JavaScript, rendering the anomalies non-threatening on their own.

The visual layer analysis corroborates this assessment. It identifies the document as a legitimate, professional book on cybersecurity titled "Cybercrime and Espionage: An Analysis of Subversive Multivector Threats". The content is academic and informational, with no signs of social engineering, phishing, or other deceptive tactics.

Considering both analyses, the structural abnormalities noted are likely due to file corruption or an unusual creation process rather than malicious intent. The absence of any executable threat, combined with the legitimate nature of the visible content, confirms the file is not malicious.

While the document presents a benign visual layer, appearing as a legitimate non-fiction book about the Syrian conflict, its internal structure contains definitive evidence of malicious intent. The text and visual content lack any typical red flags, such as suspicious links, urgency cues, or requests for user interaction, making it appear harmless to the end-user.

However, a technical analysis reveals a high-risk configuration. The PDF contains an `/OpenAction` trigger, which is designed to execute automatically as soon as the file is opened. This action is hidden within one of 127 obfuscated streams that all failed to be decoded, a strong indicator of a deliberate attempt to evade analysis. The document also embeds an exceptionally large number of URIs (884), which are not visible on the surface.

The most probable scenario is that the `/OpenAction` is designed to silently connect to one or more of the hidden URIs to deliver a secondary payload, such as malware or ransomware, or to direct the user to a phishing website. The benign appearance of the book serves as a lure to convince the user to open the file, initiating the hidden malicious process.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature fires on the presence of Base64 encoded URI prefixes (http:// and https://) across any file. The simple presence of such strings is not inherently an indicator of malicious content, but is worth further investigation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious New Instance Of An Office COM Object
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an svchost process spawning an instance of an office application. This happens when the initial word application creates an instance of one of the Office COM objects such as 'Word.Application', 'Excel.Application', etc.
This can be used by malicious actors to create malicious Office documents with macros on the fly. (See vba2clr project in the references)

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects a PDF file that contains JavaScript. JavaScript can be used to customize PDFs by implementing objects, methods, and properties. While not inherently malicious, embedding JavaScript inside of a PDF is often used for malicious purposes such as malware delivery or exploitation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The file is assessed as benign. A technical analysis of the PDF's internal structure confirms the absence of any malicious execution mechanism. No automatic action triggers, JavaScript objects, or other forms of embedded executable code were found, rendering the file inert from a technical standpoint.

The visual and textual analysis corroborates this finding, identifying the document as a legitimate, professionally formatted academic paper about Ukrainian military software. The content, language, and structure are consistent with a non-malicious research report. There are no social engineering red flags, such as urgency cues or deceptive calls to action.

The large number of URIs (430) found by the technical parser is explained by the document's content, which includes an extensive bibliography with citations to relevant and legitimate domains. The numerous decoding errors are indicative of a malformed file rather than a security threat. Both analyses independently and collectively conclude that the file poses no threat.

names: Drone Wars_ Pioneers, Killing Machines, Artificial Intelligence, and the Battle for the Future-Bombardier Books (2021).pdf
attributes.meaningful_name: Drone Wars_ Pioneers, Killing Machines, Artificial Intelligence, and the Battle for the Future-Bombardier Books (2021).pdf

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
This PDF is determined to be benign. The visual and textual analysis identifies the document as a legitimate excerpt from a professionally published book, "Drone Wars," by Seth J. Frantzman. The content, including publisher branding (Bombardier Books, Post Hill Press) and a valid domain, is authentic and lacks any phishing indicators, urgency cues, or other social engineering tactics.

The internal technical analysis supports this conclusion. Despite a large number of objects, hyperlinks (URIs), and a high rate of stream decoding errors, there is no evidence of a malicious execution chain. The file contains no JavaScript, automatic open actions, or other scripts that could pose a threat. The numerous URIs are simple hyperlinks embedded in the text, and the structural anomalies are inert, lacking any connection to a functional threat.

In synthesis, the legitimate nature of the visible content provides context for the file's complexity, and the technical inspection confirms the absence of any active malicious components. The file is a non-interactive, static document.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an Adobe Type 1 Font. The Type 1 Font Format is a standardized font format for digital imaging applications.
